title: Knowledge Nugget | India’s new Solid Waste Management Rules 2026: 10 key points for your UPSC exam
explain: Hey there! India is updating its rules on how to handle trash in 2026. They are focusing on a 'circular economy' (which just means recycling things so they can be used again and again) and using special techniques like bioremediation to clean up the environment. It's all about making sure we use more recycled plastic!

title: New Solid Waste Management Rules Notified; To Come into Force from April 1, 2026
explain: Starting April 1, 2026, new rules will help clean up not just cities, but also the areas on the edges of towns (peri-urban areas). They've also introduced something called 'Refuse Derived Fuel' (RDF)—this is basically taking non-recyclable trash like old textiles and plastic, shredding it and drying it out to use as a fuel source!

title: Methods Involved in Plastic Waste Management
explain: Ever wonder where your plastic goes? There are three main ways we deal with it: recycling (making new things from old plastic), incineration (burning it), and landfills (burying it). Recycling is the coolest because it gives plastic a second life!

title: Solid Waste Management (SWM) Rules, 2026 to come into effect from 1st April, 2026 | Current Affairs | Vision IAS
explain: Big news! The old 2016 trash rules are being replaced by the 2026 version. One of the most important changes is 'Four-Stream Segregation.' This means we need to separate our trash into four specific groups, like wet kitchen waste and dry waste (plastics/metals), to make recycling much easier.

title: Toolkit: Plastic Waste Management - Swachh Bharat Mission
explain: To clean up India, the Swachh Bharat Mission suggests a game plan: first, figure out how much plastic is being made at home, in schools, and in shops. Then, organize people to collect that plastic from every door and find a safe place in the village to store it before it gets processed.

title: Plastic Waste Management | Plastics and the Environment Series – Geneva Environment Network
explain: This isn't just an India thing—it's a world thing! The 'Basel Convention' is an international agreement where countries work together to stop plastic pollution and create global guidelines for handling plastic and electronic waste safely.

title: THE PLASTIC WASTE MANAGEMENT RULES, 2016 (as amended to date)
explain: According to these rules, your local government is the 'boss' of trash. They are responsible for setting up the bins, trucks, and factories needed to sort, move, and get rid of plastic waste in your neighborhood.
